system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
user_prompt:
Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain Maternal Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Kashmiri (code=ks, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Maternal Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jammu & Kashmir, district=Shupiyan.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Arabic script for Kashmiri (not English; not a different Indic script).

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 32
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Senior Officials of Political Party Organisations, Other
- education_level: Graduate & above
- zone: Rural
- state: Jammu & Kashmir
- district: Shupiyan
- first_language: Kashmiri
- clinical_persona_summary: Zarifa Bibi is a 32-year-old female currently married, working as a senior political party official in rural Jammu & Kashmir. She holds a graduate degree and is fluent in Kashmiri, Urdu, and English, with a background in political strategy and community mobilization. Her lifestyle includes trekking and Sufi poetry, reflecting a balance of physical activity and cultural engagement.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital X]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-X-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. ...]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|...]]
Chief complaint: <short clinical line matching assigned domain>

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Shupiyan District Hospital]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|SDH-SHU-001]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Zarifa Bibi]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-08-15]] | Age: [[AGE|32]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Senior Officials of Political Party Organisations, Other]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-0815-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ayesha Malik]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Mohammed Yousuf]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9419012345]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-SDH-045]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Shupiyan]]
Chief complaint: Routine antenatal checkup at 28 weeks gestation with mild lower back pain.
Examination: Patient is conscious, oriented, vitals stable. Fundal height 28 cm. Fetal heart rate 140 bpm. No edema or pallor. Abdominal examination reveals a single live fetus in longitudinal lie.
Investigations: Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL, Blood group O positive. Ultrasound report attached confirming gestational age and fetal well-being.
Diagnosis: Normal singleton pregnancy at 28 weeks, mild musculoskeletal backache.
Plan: Prescribed iron and folic acid tablets, advised to maintain adequate hydration and rest. Recommended gentle prenatal yoga. Follow-up in 4 weeks for routine ANC.
Date: 15 August 2024
Time: 10:30 AM

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Prescription for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Kashmiri (code=ks, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jammu & Kashmir, district=Shupiyan.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Arabic script for Kashmiri (not English; not a different Indic script).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 32
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Senior Officials of Political Party Organisations, Other
- education_level: Graduate & above
- zone: Rural
- state: Jammu & Kashmir
- district: Shupiyan
- first_language: Kashmiri
- clinical_persona_summary: Zarifa Bibi is a 32-year-old female political strategist currently married, working as a senior official in a political party organization. She holds a graduate degree and resides in rural Shupiyan, Jammu & Kashmir, where she is a native Kashmiri speaker and fluent in Urdu. Her lifestyle includes trekking in the Pir Panjal hills and a preference for traditional Kashmiri cuisine.

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|RX-1001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Rx: <drug dose frequency>  (do NOT tag drug names as entities)

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

professional_persona: Chao, a 33‑year‑old home‑based culinary and craft entrepreneur, blends their expertise in Manipuri cooking, bamboo‑weaving, and household management with a gregarious, improvisational style, though they often find it challenging to stick to strict schedules.
linguistic_persona: Chao, fluent in Manipuri (Meitei) and comfortable reading both Meitei Mayek and Bengali scripts, can navigate everyday English signage and write short notes, while they understand basic Hindi from television.
sports_persona: Chao follows NEROCA FC in the I‑League, cheers for the Indian women’s hockey squad, and stays fit with early morning walks and occasional community yoga sessions, though they rarely play organized sports themselves.
arts_persona: Chao immerses themselves in Manipuri folk dance, enjoys listening to A.R. Rahman’s film scores, sings along to Lata Mangeshkar’s timeless bhajans, and follows regional actress Bala Hijam’s movies.
travel_persona: Chao enjoys weekend trips to nearby Imphal markets and occasional trips to Shillong’s waterfalls, while they dream of a pilgrimage to Varanasi and a beach holiday in Goa.
culinary_persona: Chao loves cooking Eromba with fermented fish, Singju with mustard leaves and bamboo shoots, and sweet Chakhao kheer, while they also experiment with paneer tikka and use fresh coriander, ginger and green chilies in every dish.
persona: Chao, a vivacious 33‑year‑old village entrepreneur, blends curiosity‑driven culinary experiments with community‑centered craft sales, while they juggle worries and a penchant for spontaneous social gatherings.
cultural_background: In Bishnupur, Manipur, Chao is part of the Meitei community, which blends Hindu Vaishnavite traditions with rich indigenous customs. Rural life revolves around agriculture, family farms, and communal festivals such as Yaoshang (the Manipuri Holi) and Cheiraoba (New Year). The village’s close‑knit social fabric encourages collective activities, from folk dance rehearsals to temple celebrations, fostering a strong sense of shared identity and mutual support.
linguistic_background: Chao's mother‑tongue is Manipuri (Meitei), which she speaks fluently and can read and write using the Meitei Mayek script as well as the Bengali script taught in school. She completed middle school, giving her functional literacy in English – she can read simple signs, recipes and letters, and write everyday notes. Hindi is not a language she uses regularly, though she can understand some spoken Hindi from television and can recognise basic Devanagari letters.
skills_and_expertise: Chao excels at managing a busy household, preparing traditional Manipuri dishes, and caring for children and elders. Her strong interpersonal skills, honed by her talkative and sociable nature, make her an effective mediator in family matters and community gatherings. She is adept at sewing, weaving bamboo crafts, and coordinating local festivals, often improvising solutions under time pressure. Additionally, her curiosity drives her to experiment with new recipes and simple DIY projects, while her practical mindset ensures they are functional and affordable.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Household management", "Manipuri culinary arts", "Childcare", "Elderly care", "Sewing and textile crafts", "Bamboo craft making", "Community festival coordination", "Conflict mediation", "Creative problem solving", "DIY project experimentation"]
hobbies_and_interests: Chao loves spending her free time socialising with neighbours over tea, dancing to traditional Manipuri folk songs, and experimenting with new recipes in her kitchen. She enjoys tending a small vegetable garden, weaving bamboo mats, and listening to devotional music on the radio. On weekends she often visits the local market to browse handicrafts, watches Bollywood and regional movies, and reads stories from the Mahabharata and local legends.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Socializing over tea with neighbours", "Traditional Manipuri folk dancing", "Experimenting with new recipes", "Vegetable gardening", "Bamboo weaving", "Listening to devotional music", "Visiting local markets", "Watching Bollywood and regional movies", "Reading mythological stories (Mahabharata, local legends)"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Chao aims to turn her expertise in Manipuri cooking and handicrafts into a small home‑based enterprise that supplies ready‑made meals and handcrafted items to nearby towns. She wishes to pursue a short certification in basic accounting and digital marketing to manage the venture effectively. Over the next few years, she hopes to mentor other rural women, organize skill‑sharing workshops, and eventually facilitate a community cooperative that empowers households financially. Simultaneously, she wants to improve her English proficiency to navigate government schemes and support her children's higher education.
persona_clinical_summary: A 33-year-old female from rural Manipur, currently married, with a middle school education, who speaks Manipuri and Bengali. She is a homemaker and retired, living in the Bishnupur district. Clinically, she has a sedentary lifestyle with occasional community yoga and walks, and a diet rich in traditional Manipuri foods like Eromba and Singju.

  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
user_prompt:
Write one complete Lab Report for clinical domain Oncology / Complex Chronic Care.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Manipuri (code=mni, script=Meitei). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Oncology / Complex Chronic Care — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Manipur, district=Bishnupur.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Meitei script for Manipuri (not English; not a different Indic script).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 33
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Middle
- zone: Rural
- state: Manipur
- district: Bishnupur
- first_language: Manipuri
- clinical_persona_summary: A 33-year-old female from rural Manipur, currently married, with a middle school education, who speaks Manipuri and Bengali. She is a homemaker and retired, living in the Bishnupur district. Clinically, she has a sedentary lifestyle with occasional community yoga and walks, and a diet rich in traditional Manipuri foods like Eromba and Singju.

Document format guide for Lab Report (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Lab report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] MRN [[MRN|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|41]] [[GENDER|Male]] Ordered by [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Results table (analyte values NOT tagged as PHI entities).

MANDATORY inline entities (10 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- STUDENT_ID — Student ID e.g. [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

পরীক্ষার বিবরণ:
পরীক্ষার ধরন: সি.ই.এ (কার্সিনো এমব্রায়োনিক অ্যান্টিজেন)
রেফারেন্স রেঞ্জ: <5.0 ng/mL
ফলাফল: 12.5 ng/mL
ব্যাখ্যা: সি.ই.এ-এর মাত্রা বৃদ্ধি পেয়েছে, যা গ্যাস্ট্রোইনটেস্টাইনাল ম্যালিগন্যান্সির সম্ভাবনা নির্দেশ করতে পারে।
অতিরিক্ত পরীক্ষা:
পরীক্ষার ধরন: সি.এ 19-9 (কার্সিনো এমব্রায়োনিক অ্যান্টিজেন 19-9)
রেফারেন্স রেঞ্জ: <37 U/mL
ফলাফল: 85.2 U/mL
ব্যাখ্যা: সি.এ 19-9-এর মাত্রা বৃদ্ধি পেয়েছে, যা অগ্ন্যাশয় বা পিত্তনালীর ক্যান্সারের সম্ভাবনা নির্দেশ করতে পারে।
ক্লিনিকাল মন্তব্য:
রোগীর পেটে ব্যথা এবং ওজন হ্রাসের ইতিহাস রয়েছে। প্রাথমিক পরীক্ষার জন্য গ্যাস্ট্রোইনটেস্টাইনাল এন্ডোস্কোপি এবং কোলনোস্কোপি করা হয়েছে।
পরিকল্পনা:
1. গ্যাস্ট্রোইনটেস্টাইনাল এন্ডোস্কোপি এবং কোলনোস্কোপির ফলাফল পর্যালোচনা করা।
2. ইমেজিং স্টাডিজ (সিটি স্ক্যান) করা।
3. অনকোলজি বিভাগে রেফার করা।
4. বায়োপসি করা।
5. চিকিৎসা শুরু করা।
স্বাক্ষর:
ডাঃ আর. কে. সিং
এম.ডি, জেনারেল মেডিসিন

judge_reasoning: Prose entirely in Bengali script/language instead of required Meitei script for Manipuri; all other elements (tags, content, persona fit) acceptable.

professional_persona: Chao, a 33‑year‑old home‑based culinary and craft entrepreneur, blends their expertise in Manipuri cooking, bamboo‑weaving, and household management with a gregarious, improvisational style, though they often find it challenging to stick to strict schedules.
linguistic_persona: Chao, fluent in Manipuri (Meitei) and comfortable reading both Meitei Mayek and Bengali scripts, can navigate everyday English signage and write short notes, while they understand basic Hindi from television.
sports_persona: Chao follows NEROCA FC in the I‑League, cheers for the Indian women’s hockey squad, and stays fit with early morning walks and occasional community yoga sessions, though they rarely play organized sports themselves.
arts_persona: Chao immerses themselves in Manipuri folk dance, enjoys listening to A.R. Rahman’s film scores, sings along to Lata Mangeshkar’s timeless bhajans, and follows regional actress Bala Hijam’s movies.
travel_persona: Chao enjoys weekend trips to nearby Imphal markets and occasional trips to Shillong’s waterfalls, while they dream of a pilgrimage to Varanasi and a beach holiday in Goa.
culinary_persona: Chao loves cooking Eromba with fermented fish, Singju with mustard leaves and bamboo shoots, and sweet Chakhao kheer, while they also experiment with paneer tikka and use fresh coriander, ginger and green chilies in every dish.
persona: Chao, a vivacious 33‑year‑old village entrepreneur, blends curiosity‑driven culinary experiments with community‑centered craft sales, while they juggle worries and a penchant for spontaneous social gatherings.
cultural_background: In Bishnupur, Manipur, Chao is part of the Meitei community, which blends Hindu Vaishnavite traditions with rich indigenous customs. Rural life revolves around agriculture, family farms, and communal festivals such as Yaoshang (the Manipuri Holi) and Cheiraoba (New Year). The village’s close‑knit social fabric encourages collective activities, from folk dance rehearsals to temple celebrations, fostering a strong sense of shared identity and mutual support.
linguistic_background: Chao's mother‑tongue is Manipuri (Meitei), which she speaks fluently and can read and write using the Meitei Mayek script as well as the Bengali script taught in school. She completed middle school, giving her functional literacy in English – she can read simple signs, recipes and letters, and write everyday notes. Hindi is not a language she uses regularly, though she can understand some spoken Hindi from television and can recognise basic Devanagari letters.
skills_and_expertise: Chao excels at managing a busy household, preparing traditional Manipuri dishes, and caring for children and elders. Her strong interpersonal skills, honed by her talkative and sociable nature, make her an effective mediator in family matters and community gatherings. She is adept at sewing, weaving bamboo crafts, and coordinating local festivals, often improvising solutions under time pressure. Additionally, her curiosity drives her to experiment with new recipes and simple DIY projects, while her practical mindset ensures they are functional and affordable.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Household management", "Manipuri culinary arts", "Childcare", "Elderly care", "Sewing and textile crafts", "Bamboo craft making", "Community festival coordination", "Conflict mediation", "Creative problem solving", "DIY project experimentation"]
hobbies_and_interests: Chao loves spending her free time socialising with neighbours over tea, dancing to traditional Manipuri folk songs, and experimenting with new recipes in her kitchen. She enjoys tending a small vegetable garden, weaving bamboo mats, and listening to devotional music on the radio. On weekends she often visits the local market to browse handicrafts, watches Bollywood and regional movies, and reads stories from the Mahabharata and local legends.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Socializing over tea with neighbours", "Traditional Manipuri folk dancing", "Experimenting with new recipes", "Vegetable gardening", "Bamboo weaving", "Listening to devotional music", "Visiting local markets", "Watching Bollywood and regional movies", "Reading mythological stories (Mahabharata, local legends)"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Chao aims to turn her expertise in Manipuri cooking and handicrafts into a small home‑based enterprise that supplies ready‑made meals and handcrafted items to nearby towns. She wishes to pursue a short certification in basic accounting and digital marketing to manage the venture effectively. Over the next few years, she hopes to mentor other rural women, organize skill‑sharing workshops, and eventually facilitate a community cooperative that empowers households financially. Simultaneously, she wants to improve her English proficiency to navigate government schemes and support her children's higher education.
persona_clinical_summary: A 33-year-old female from rural Manipur, currently married, with a middle education level, works as a homemaker. Her first language is Manipuri, and she is part of the Meitei community. Clinically, she has a diet rich in fermented foods and traditional dishes, and she engages in community-based activities.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
user_prompt:
Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Manipuri (code=mni, script=Meitei). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Manipur, district=Bishnupur.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Meitei script for Manipuri (not English; not a different Indic script).

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 33
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Middle
- zone: Rural
- state: Manipur
- district: Bishnupur
- first_language: Manipuri
- clinical_persona_summary: A 33-year-old female from rural Manipur, currently married, with a middle education level, works as a homemaker. Her first language is Manipuri, and she is part of the Meitei community. Clinically, she has a diet rich in fermented foods and traditional dishes, and she engages in community-based activities.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital X]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-X-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. ...]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|...]]
Chief complaint: <short clinical line matching assigned domain>

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

generated_text_en:
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Bishnupur District Hospital]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|BDH-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Thokchom Sanjita Devi]] | DOB [[DOB|1991-07-22]] | Age: [[AGE|33]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Homemaker]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-0815-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. L. Renu]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Thokchom Sanjoy Singh]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-BDH-042]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Bishnupur]]
Chief complaint: Persistent cough with yellowish sputum for 10 days, associated with low-grade fever and generalized body ache.

History of present illness: Patient reports a productive cough that started 10 days ago, initially dry and now with yellowish sputum. She has been experiencing fever in the evenings, peaking around 99.5°F, and feels weak with muscle pain throughout her body. She has been taking over-the-counter paracetamol and cough syrup with minimal relief.

Past medical history: No known chronic illnesses. No history of tuberculosis or asthma.

Physical examination:
- General: Appears mildly pale, conscious, and oriented.
- Respiratory: Tachypneic with respiratory rate of 22 breaths/min. Chest expansion is reduced bilaterally. Percussion note is dull in the right infra-axillary area. Breath sounds are vesicular with scattered rhonchi. No wheeze or crepitations heard.
- Cardiovascular: Pulse rate 98/min, regular. Blood pressure 120/80 mmHg. No murmurs.
- Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. Liver and spleen not palpable.
- Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema.

Investigations:
- CBC: Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL, WBC count 12,000/cmm with neutrophilia.
- Chest X-ray: Right lower zone consolidation suggestive of pneumonia.

Diagnosis: Community-acquired pneumonia, right lower lobe.

Treatment plan:
1. Start on oral Azithromycin 500 mg once daily for 3 days.
2. Paracetamol 500 mg for fever and body ache as needed.
3. Adequate rest and hydration.
4. Follow-up in OPD in 3 days or sooner if symptoms worsen.
5. Advise sputum for AFB testing to rule out tuberculosis.

Patient counseled regarding the diagnosis, treatment plan, and importance of completing the course of antibiotics. She was advised to return immediately if she develops shortness of breath or high-grade fever.

sports_persona: Chao follows NEROCA FC in the I‑League, cheers for the Indian women’s hockey squad, and stays fit with early morning walks and occasional community yoga sessions, though they rarely play organized sports themselves.

persona: Chao, a vivacious 33‑year‑old village entrepreneur, blends curiosity‑driven culinary experiments with community‑centered craft sales, while they juggle worries and a penchant for spontaneous social gatherings.
cultural_background: In Bishnupur, Manipur, Chao is part of the Meitei community, which blends Hindu Vaishnavite traditions with rich indigenous customs. Rural life revolves around agriculture, family farms, and communal festivals such as Yaoshang (the Manipuri Holi) and Cheiraoba (New Year). The village’s close‑knit social fabric encourages collective activities, from folk dance rehearsals to temple celebrations, fostering a strong sense of shared identity and mutual support.
linguistic_background: Chao's mother‑tongue is Manipuri (Meitei), which she speaks fluently and can read and write using the Meitei Mayek script as well as the Bengali script taught in school. She completed middle school, giving her functional literacy in English – she can read simple signs, recipes and letters, and write everyday notes. Hindi is not a language she uses regularly, though she can understand some spoken Hindi from television and can recognise basic Devanagari letters.
skills_and_expertise: Chao excels at managing a busy household, preparing traditional Manipuri dishes, and caring for children and elders. Her strong interpersonal skills, honed by her talkative and sociable nature, make her an effective mediator in family matters and community gatherings. She is adept at sewing, weaving bamboo crafts, and coordinating local festivals, often improvising solutions under time pressure. Additionally, her curiosity drives her to experiment with new recipes and simple DIY projects, while her practical mindset ensures they are functional and affordable.

career_goals_and_ambitions: Chao aims to turn her expertise in Manipuri cooking and handicrafts into a small home‑based enterprise that supplies ready‑made meals and handcrafted items to nearby towns. - ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

নংথোমবাম বিমল ৩০৩ [[GENDER|Female]] | ২৮ সপ্তাহ গর্ভকালীন সময়ের জন্য প্রসবপূর্ব পরীক্ষা

প্রধান অভিযোগ: রোগী নিয়মিত প্রসবপূর্ব পরীক্ষার জন্য এসেছেন। ২ দিন ধরে পেটের নিচের অংশে সামান্য অস্বস্তি হচ্ছে, জ্বর বা রক্তপাত নেই। গত সপ্তাহের তুলনায় ভ্রূণের নড়াচড়া কমে গেছে বলে মনে করছেন।

বর্তমান অসুস্থতার ইতিহাস: திருமதி বিমল, লামলাই গ্রামের একজন গৃহিণী, বিষ্ণুপুর জেলার বাসিন্দা, ২৮ সপ্তাহের প্রসবপূর্ব পরীক্ষার জন্য এসেছেন। তিনি পেটের নিচের অংশে মাঝে মাঝে হালকা ব্যথা অনুভব করছেন, যা তিনি ক্রমবর্ধমান জরায়ুর কারণে হচ্ছে বলে মনে করেন। তিনি যোনিপথে রক্তপাত, তরল নিঃসরণ বা জ্বরের কথা অস্বীকার করেছেন। তিনি উল্লেখ করেছেন যে আজ শিশুর নড়াচড়া কম ঘনঘন হচ্ছে।

প্রসূতি ইতিহাস: Gravida 2, Para 1। ৩ বছর আগে একই পি.এইচ.সি-তে স্বাভাবিক যোনিপথে প্রসব হয়েছে। শেষ ঋতুস্রাব প্রায় ১৮০ দিন আগে হয়েছিল, যা ২৮ সপ্তাহের গর্ভকালীন সময়ের সাথে সামঞ্জস্যপূর্ণ। ৪ সপ্তাহ আগে করা আল্ট্রাসাউন্ড স্ক্যানে একটি সজীব ভ্রূণ সেফালিক প্রেজেন্টেশনে দেখা গেছে।

পরীক্ষার ফলাফল:
- সাধারণ: সতর্ক, সুপুষ্ট, সামান্য উদ্বিগ্ন দেখাচ্ছেন।
- ভাইটাল সাইনস: বি.পি ১১০/৭০ mmHg, পালস ৮৮/মিনিট, তাপমাত্রা ৩৬.৮°C, ঘরে থাকা বাতাসে SpO2 ৯৮%।
- পেট: জরায়ু গর্ভবতী, ফান্ডাল হাইট ২৮ সেমি। পেটের নিচের অংশে গভীর চাপে সামান্য ব্যথা। ভ্রূণের হৃদস্পন্দন ১৪০ bpm, নিয়মিত। ভ্রূণের নড়াচড়া অনুভব করা যাচ্ছে কিন্তু কমে গেছে।
- স্পেকুলাম পরীক্ষা: জরায়ুমুখ লম্বা, বন্ধ এবং পেছনের দিকে। কোনো রক্তপাত বা নিঃসরণ দেখা যায়নি।

তদন্ত:
- প্রস্রাব পরীক্ষা: স্বাভাবিক
- রক্তের গ্রুপ: O Positive
- Hb: ১১.২ g/dL
- রক্তে শর্করা (উপবাস): ৮৫ mg/dL

মূল্যায়ন:
১. ২৮ সপ্তাহের প্রসবপূর্ব পরীক্ষা
২. পেটের নিচের অংশে সামান্য অস্বস্তি, সম্ভবত শারীরবৃত্তীয়
৩. রোগী ভ্রূণের নড়াচড়া কমে যাওয়ার কথা জানিয়েছেন
৪. সামান্য রক্তাল্পতা (Hb ১১.২ g/dL)

পরিকল্পনা:
১. ভ্রূণের নড়াচড়ার স্বাভাবিক পরিবর্তন সম্পর্কে রোগীকে পরামর্শ দেওয়া হয়েছে এবং কোনো উদ্বেগজনক উপসর্গ দেখা দিলে অবিলম্বে জানানোর পরামর্শ দেওয়া হয়েছে।
২. প্রতিদিন আয়রন এবং ফলিক অ্যাসিড ট্যাবলেট খাওয়ার পরামর্শ দেওয়া হয়েছে।
৩. তরল গ্রহণ এবং আয়রন সমৃদ্ধ খাবার বাড়ানোর পরামর্শ দেওয়া হয়েছে।
৪. ২ সপ্তাহ পরে পরবর্তী প্রসবপূর্ব পরীক্ষার সময় নির্ধারণ করা হয়েছে।
৫. ভ্রূণের সুস্থতা মূল্যায়নের জন্য বিস্তারিত আল্ট্রাসাউন্ড স্ক্যানের উদ্দেশ্যে বিষ্ণুপুর জেলা হাসপাতালে পাঠানো হয়েছে।

PHC register — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Primary Health Centre, Bishnupur]] Village [[VILLAGE|Lamlai]] District [[DISTRICT|Bishnupur]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Nongthombam Bimol]] [[AGE|33]] [[GENDER|Female]] | Antenatal check-up for 28 weeks gestation

Chief Complaint: Patient presents for routine antenatal visit. Reports mild lower abdominal discomfort for 2 days, no fever or bleeding. Feels fetal movements are reduced compared to last week.

History of Present Illness: Mrs. Bimol, a 33-year-old homemaker from Lamlai village, Bishnupur district, is here for her 28-week antenatal check-up. She mentions intermittent dull aches in the lower abdomen, which she attributes to the growing uterus. She denies any vaginal bleeding, leakage of fluid, or fever. She notes that the baby's movements seem less frequent today.

Obstetric History: Gravida 2, Para 1. Previous delivery was a normal vaginal delivery at the same PHC 3 years ago. Last menstrual period was approximately 180 days ago, consistent with a 28-week gestation. Ultrasound scan from 4 weeks ago confirmed a single live fetus in cephalic presentation.

Examination Findings:
- General: Alert, well-nourished, appears mildly anxious.
- Vital Signs: BP 110/70 mmHg, Pulse 88/min, Temperature 36.8°C, SpO2 98% on room air.
- Abdomen: Uterus is gravid, fundal height 28 cm. Mild tenderness on deep palpation in the suprapubic region. Fetal heart rate 140 bpm, regular. Fetal movements are palpable but reduced.
- Speculum Examination: Cervix is long, closed, and posterior. No bleeding or discharge noted.

Investigations:
- Urine Routine: Normal
- Blood Group: O Positive
- Hb: 11.2 g/dL
- Blood Sugar (Fasting): 85 mg/dL

Assessment:
1. 28 weeks Antenatal Visit
2. Mild lower abdominal discomfort, likely physiological
3. Reduced fetal movements reported by patient
4. Mild anemia (Hb 11.2 g/dL)

Plan:
1. Counselled patient on normal variations in fetal movements and advised to report any further decrease or concerning symptoms immediately.
2. Prescribed iron and folic acid tablets to be taken daily.
3. Advised to increase fluid intake and dietary iron.
4. Scheduled next antenatal visit in 2 weeks.
5. Referred to the District Hospital, Bishnupur for a detailed ultrasound scan to assess fetal well-being due to reported reduced movements.

persona: Swarna Kanr, a vivacious 73‑year‑old keeper of oral tales and Phulkari art, balances frugal living with generous festival splurges, manages health aches with herbal remedies, and radiates curiosity through endless storytelling.
cultural_background: Swarna hails from a small village near Rupnagar in the rural heartland of Punjab. She belongs to a Punjabi Hindu family that has lived in the region for generations, steeped in Punjabi folk traditions, agricultural life, and community festivals such as Baisakhi, Lohri and Teeyan. Her household follows joint‑family customs, with strong emphasis on hospitality, respect for elders and the preservation of oral history.
linguistic_background: Swarna's mother tongue is Punjabi; she speaks Punjabi fluently and understands its nuances, idioms and proverbs. Because she never attended school, she cannot read or write Gurmukhi, Hindi or English. She can follow simple spoken Hindi in daily conversations and understands basic instructions from television or neighbours, but she does not read Hindi script. Her linguistic skills are rooted in oral tradition, storytelling and memorised devotional chants.
skills_and_expertise: Swarna excels in managing a Punjabi household, preparing traditional dishes such as makki di roti, sarson da saag, and lassi. She is proficient in Phulkari embroidery, creating intricate needlework for family shawls. - VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.

- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.
user_prompt:
Write one complete ER Triage & Nursing Notes for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Punjabi (code=pa, script=Gurmukhi). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Punjab, district=Rupnagar.
SCRIPT ANCHOR: after translation the body must be in Gurmukhi script for Punjabi (not English; not a different Indic script).

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once in the arrival/RTA/ambulance line. Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 73
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Punjab
- district: Rupnagar
- first_language: Punjabi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 73-year-old female from rural Punjab, currently married, with no formal occupation and illiterate education. She speaks Punjabi as her first language and follows a traditional lifestyle with limited formal health engagement. Her daily routine includes managing household tasks and community activities, with a diet rich in local staples.

Document format guide for ER Triage & Nursing Notes (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|22]] [[GENDER|Male]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|...]]
Arrived by ambulance / RTA vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|PB10AB1234]] (exactly once).
Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|...]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Vitals / acuity: <short notes>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION — Vehicle Registration Number e.g. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

[[PATIENT_NAME|ਜਸਵਿੰਦਰ ਕੌਰ]] [[AGE|73]] [[GENDER|Female]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|04]]

professional_persona: Ahmed Hasan, a budding Sanskrit scholar and aspiring academic researcher, leverages their deep mastery of Vedic texts and precise Devanagari calligraphy to craft scholarly articles, while their relaxed, flexible approach often leads them to juggle study sessions with spontaneous creative projects.
linguistic_persona: Ahmed Hasan, fluent in Sanskrit with the ability to read, write, and recite ancient verses, possesses functional English for academic work and basic Hindi for everyday chats, yet often switches to Sanskrit when discussing cultural topics.
sports_persona: Ahmed Hasan enjoys informal cricket matches at the local park with friends and cheers for the Delhi Capitals in the IPL, yet their sporadic fitness routine sometimes clashes with exam pressure.
arts_persona: Ahmed Hasan crafts intricate Devanagari calligraphy sketches, immerses in Qawwali and Sufi melodies, and occasionally attends local cultural festivals, letting these artistic pursuits balance their academic intensity.
travel_persona: Ahmed Hasan prefers budget-friendly weekend getaways to historical spots like Lucknow’s Bara Imambara and Varanasi’s ghats, dreaming of a pilgrimage to Ajmer’s Dargah once finances allow, but often postpones travel due to exam schedules.
culinary_persona: Ahmed Hasan relishes Kanpur’s spicy aloo tikki chaat, succulent kebabs, and homemade vegetable pulao, occasionally attempting simple recipes like egg bhurji, but their culinary ambitions are limited by a modest kitchen and frequent late-night gaming snacks.
persona: Ahmed Hasan, a 19‑year‑old Sanskrit enthusiast with a penchant for calligraphy and Qawwali, juggles academic dreams and anxiety‑driven yoga sessions, while often forgetting deadlines in favour of spontaneous street‑food cravings.
cultural_background: Ahmed comes from an urban Muslim family in Kanpur Nagar, Uttar Pradesh, where a deep appreciation for classical Indian culture coexists with Islamic traditions. His household places a strong emphasis on learning Sanskrit as a link to the subcontinent’s literary heritage, while also observing the rituals and community life of the Muslim ummah.
linguistic_background: In terms of language, Ahmed’s mother tongue is Sanskrit, which he reads, writes and speaks fluently thanks to his family’s scholarly tradition and his own schooling up to the 10th standard. He has functional proficiency in English, enough to understand textbooks and communicate in urban settings, while his Hindi skill remains basic, limited to everyday interactions and informal listening.
skills_and_expertise: Ahmed’s expertise lies primarily in classical language studies and related cultural skills. He is proficient in reading, interpreting, and reciting Sanskrit texts, including Vedic hymns and epics. His calligraphic skills enable him to write Devanagari script with precision. He also possesses basic digital literacy, such as using a smartphone and internet for educational purposes, and is adept at modest home management tasks. His moderate interpersonal communication reflects his balanced social nature, and his emotional reactivity drives him to practice yoga and meditation for self‑regulation.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Sanskrit reading', 'Sanskrit recitation', 'Devanagari calligraphy', 'Interpretation of Vedic texts', 'Basic digital literacy', 'Smartphone usage for learning', 'Home management tasks', 'Moderate interpersonal communication']
hobbies_and_interests: Ahmed enjoys reading Sanskrit epics and philosophical texts, listening to Qawwali and Sufi music, practicing yoga and meditation for emotional balance, sketching calligraphic art, playing cricket informally with friends in the local park, exploring street food in Kanpur, and experimenting with simple cooking recipes at home. He also spends time browsing documentaries on history and culture, and occasionally engages in online gaming as a way to unwind.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Reading Sanskrit epics', 'Listening to Qawwali and Sufi music', 'Yoga and meditation', 'Devanagari calligraphy sketches', 'Playing informal cricket', 'Exploring Kanpur street food', 'Simple home cooking experiments', 'Watching history and culture documentaries', 'Online gaming']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Ahmed aspires to pursue higher education in Sanskrit literature, aiming for a Bachelor’s degree followed by a Master’s to become a teacher or researcher in a university or cultural institution. He also wishes to develop content that bridges Islamic and Indian classical traditions, possibly through a YouTube channel or community workshops. He hopes to secure a stable job in academia or heritage preservation while managing his emotional well‑being and maintaining flexibility in his schedule.
persona_clinical_summary: A 19‑year‑old male from Kanpur Nagar, Uttar Pradesh, currently not married, with a matriculation education and no formal occupation. He resides in an urban setting and speaks Sanskrit as his first language, with functional English proficiency. His lifestyle includes occasional anxiety‑driven yoga, frequent consumption of street food, and a cultural background that blends Islamic traditions with an appreciation for classical Indian arts.

[[PATIENT_NAME|Aarav Sharma]] [[AGE|19]] [[GENDER|Male]] | Patient presented with acute right lower quadrant abdominal pain and fever.

[[PATIENT_NAME|Aarav Sharma]] is a 19-year-old male from Bilhaur, Kanpur Nagar, Uttar Pradesh. He is unmarried and currently not employed. His education level is matriculation. He resides in an urban area and his first language is Sanskrit.

Chief Complaint: The patient reports a sudden onset of sharp, localized pain in the right lower quadrant of the abdomen, which began approximately 12 hours ago. The pain is constant and radiates to the right flank. He also reports a low-grade fever, nausea, and one episode of non-bilious vomiting. He denies any change in bowel habits or urinary symptoms.

History of Present Illness: The patient is a 19-year-old male who presents with a 12-hour history of acute right lower quadrant abdominal pain. The pain started suddenly and is described as sharp and constant, rated 8/10 in severity. He reports associated fever, nausea, and a single episode of vomiting. He denies any recent trauma, foreign travel, or sick contacts. His last meal was street food from a local vendor about 8 hours prior to symptom onset.

Physical Examination:
- General: Alert and oriented, appears uncomfortable, lying in a guarded position with the right hip flexed.
- Vital Signs: Temperature: 101.2°F, Pulse: 98/min, Respiratory Rate: 18/min, Blood Pressure: 120/80 mmHg, SpO2: 98% on room air.
- Abdomen: Soft, with marked tenderness and guarding in the right lower quadrant. Rebound tenderness is present. Bowel sounds are hypoactive. No masses or organomegaly palpated.
- Other Systems: Unremarkable.

Investigations:
- Complete Blood Count (CBC): Hemoglobin: 14.5 g/dL, White Blood Cell count: 14,500/mm³ with a neutrophilic predominance.
- C-Reactive Protein (CRP): 12.5 mg/L (Elevated).
- Urine Routine: No significant abnormalities.

Assessment:
1. Acute Appendicitis, likely perforated.
2. Acute gastroenteritis.

Plan:
1. The patient was advised to proceed to the District Hospital Kanpur for further evaluation and surgical management.
2. Intravenous fluids were initiated.
3. Analgesics and antipyretics were administered.
4. Blood samples were sent for cross-matching.
5. The patient's [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]] was informed and will accompany the patient to the hospital.
6. The patient's [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]] was contacted for confirmation.
7. The patient's [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]] and [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]] were verified for records.
8. The patient's [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]] was noted for digital health records.

[[PATIENT_NAME|Aarav Sharma]] was discharged with instructions to report to the District Hospital Kanpur for an emergency appendectomy. Follow-up will be coordinated by the ASHA worker.

Patient Meena Devi, a 32-year-old female telemarketing sales person from rural Dumka, was admitted to our facility following a road traffic accident. She reports that she was walking near her home in the village of [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]] when she was struck by a speeding motorcycle. The right lower limb shows a 10 cm laceration over the lateral aspect of the mid-calf with underlying bone exposure. There is significant swelling and deformity of the ankle joint. Radiographs of the right leg reveal a comminuted fracture of the distal fibula and a fracture of the lateral malleolus. The patient has intact distal pulses and sensation in the foot.

Given the open nature of the fracture and the associated joint involvement, we recommend urgent surgical debridement and open reduction with internal fixation (ORIF) of the right ankle. The patient has been counseled regarding the procedure, risks, and benefits. She has provided consent for the surgery.

For further management, we are referring the patient to your surgical department for definitive treatment. Her [[MRN|MRN-2024-0918-001]] and [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0918-001]] are attached for your records. Please contact us at [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] for any further information.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.
You are capable across all 22 official Indian languages + English: write fluent clinical prose in the requested native script.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER
